title: The cancellation norm and the geometry of bi-invariant word metrics
overall_summary: The paper studies the cancellation length of words in a finitely normally generated group and proves that the induced cancellation norm on group elements is exactly the bi-invariant word norm associated to the generating set. Its most relevant free-group contribution is a recursive first-letter formula for the cancellation norm: in a free group, an optimal trivialisation of xw either deletes the initial x or pairs it with an inverse occurrence x^{-1} in w, splitting the problem into the two intervening subwords. This yields an O(N^3)-time, O(N^2)-memory dynamic programming algorithm for the cancellation norm of a word of ordinary length N in a free group. For the original matching-complex problem, the paper is useful mainly as an algebraic source of cancellation pairings and interval-splitting induction: in the norm-zero case of a freely trivial word, the recursion forces an inverse-letter pair whose removal separates the word into two freely trivial subwords.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a group finitely normally generated by a symmetric set S. For a word w = s1 ... sn in the alphabet S, define its cancellation length |w|× to be the least k such that deleting k letters from w leaves a word representing the identity in G. For g in G, define the cancellation norm |g|× to be the minimum of |w|× over all words w representing g. Let ∥g∥ be the bi-invariant word norm associated to S, equivalently the least k such that g is a product of k conjugates of elements of S. Then |g|× = ∥g∥ for every g in G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the inequality |g|× ≤ ∥g∥, write g as a product of k conjugates, g = product_{i=1}^k wi^{-1} si wi with si in S. In the concatenated word representing this product, deleting the distinguished letters si leaves a product of conjugating words and their inverses, hence a word trivial in G; thus k deletions suffice. For the reverse inequality, suppose a word representing g has a trivialising sequence of k deleted letters. Write the word in the form u0 s1 u1 ... sk uk, where deleting the letters s1,...,sk leaves u0 u1 ... uk = 1 in G. Set Wi = ui ui+1 ... uk for i = 1,...,k. Since u0 = (u1 ... uk)^{-1}, a telescoping calculation gives g = product_{i=1}^k Wi^{-1} si Wi. Thus g is a product of k conjugates of generators, so ∥g∥ ≤ k. Minimising over trivialising sequences gives ∥g∥ ≤ |g|×.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group presentation. A defining relation v = w in R is called balanced if, whenever a word v̄ is obtained from v by deleting k letters, there are k letters in w whose deletion gives a word w̄ with v̄ = w̄ in G. If v = w is a balanced relation, then for any words x and y in the alphabet S, the cancellation lengths satisfy |x v y|× = |x w y|×.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The idea is to transfer any deletion pattern through the balanced relation without changing the number of deleted letters. Given a trivialising sequence for x v y, restrict attention to how many letters it deletes from the subword v. By balancedness, delete the same number of letters from w so that the remaining subword represents the same group element as the remaining part of v. Keeping the deletion choices in x and y unchanged produces a trivialising sequence of the same length for x w y. Applying the same argument in the reverse direction for the same equality gives equality of cancellation lengths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a presentation all of whose defining relations are balanced. If u and v are two words in the alphabet S representing the same element g in G, then |u|× = |v|×. Consequently, for such a presentation, the cancellation norm |g|× is equal to the cancellation length of any word representing g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It is enough to show that applying one defining relation inside an arbitrary context preserves cancellation length. Suppose one word has the form x y and the other has the form x r^{-1} t y, where r = t is a defining relation. The paper computes |x r^{-1} t y|× = |x r^{-1} r y|× by Lemma 2.2, because t may be replaced by r without changing cancellation length. The word r^{-1} r is freely trivial, so |x r^{-1} r y|× = |x y|×. Any two words representing the same group element are connected by a finite sequence of such applications of defining relations in contexts, so cancellation length is constant along the sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 2.3 and Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Coxeter groups and right-angled Artin groups admit presentations in which all defining relations are balanced. In particular, if G is either a Coxeter group or a right-angled Artin group, then the cancellation norm of an element of G does not depend on the chosen representing word.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a right-angled Artin group, use the standard presentation with commutation relations st = ts for the commuting generators; deleting any number of letters from one side can be matched on the other side because the two sides contain the same letters in the opposite order. For a Coxeter group, the paper notes a presentation with relations s = s^{-1} and braid-type alternating relations st ... s = ts ... t, equivalently (st)^n = (ts)^n in the relevant cases; these are balanced for the same reason, because corresponding sides contain compatible alternating multisets of generators. Proposition 2.5 then implies that cancellation length is invariant under changing the representative word, and hence the cancellation norm is realised by every representative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x be a generator of a free group F_m, and let w be a word in F_m. With ∥·∥ denoting the conjugation-invariant word norm, equivalently the cancellation norm by Proposition 2.1, one has ∥x w∥ = min(1 + ∥w∥, min{∥u∥ + ∥v∥ : w = u x^{-1} v}), where the inner minimum ranges over all decompositions of the word w around an occurrence of the letter x^{-1}. If no occurrence of x^{-1} appears in w, the inner minimum is absent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, there are two constructions. First, delete the initial x and then use an optimal trivialising sequence for w; this gives length 1 + ∥w∥. Second, if w = u x^{-1} v, delete optimal trivialising sequences in u and in v. The remaining word is x x^{-1}, which freely cancels, so ∥xw∥ ≤ ∥u∥ + ∥v∥ for every such decomposition. For the lower bound, take a minimal trivialising sequence for xw. If the initial x is among the deleted letters, then the remaining deleted letters must trivialise w, giving length at least 1 + ∥w∥. If the initial x is not deleted, then in the freely trivial word left after deleting the minimal sequence, that initial x must cancel with some undeleted occurrence of x^{-1} in w. Write w = u x^{-1} v at that occurrence. Because the group is free, no relation can mix letters across this cancellation except by first reducing the prefix u to the identity and independently reducing the suffix v to the identity. Thus the deleted letters before that x^{-1} form a minimal trivialising sequence for u, and the deleted letters after it form a minimal trivialising sequence for v, giving length at least ∥u∥ + ∥v∥. This proves the displayed recurrence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5 / Section 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a word w of ordinary length N in a nonabelian finitely generated free group, there is an algorithm computing the conjugation-invariant word length, equivalently the cancellation norm, of w. The algorithm runs in O(N^3) time and uses O(N^2) memory.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The algorithm is dynamic programming on connected subwords of a reduced input word. Suppose v is a reduced connected subword of ordinary length k and the norms of all proper connected subwords of v are already known. Apply Lemma 2.10 to the first letter of v: scan v from left to right to find all occurrences of the inverse of the first letter, and for each such occurrence use the stored values of the two resulting shorter connected subwords. Taking the minimum over these possibilities and over deleting the first letter computes ∥v∥ in time O(k). Now compute all subwords in increasing order of length: lengths 1 and 2 are initialised directly for reduced words, then length 3, length 4, and so on up to N. There are at most N connected subwords of each length k, so the total time is bounded by a constant times sum_{k=1}^N N k = O(N^3). The algorithm stores only the norm values for connected subwords, of which there are O(N^2), giving the stated memory bound.

proof_techniques.name: Trivialising sequences as deletion certificates
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces geometric or metric questions about the bi-invariant word norm by combinatorics of deleting letters from a word. A trivialising sequence is a sequence of indices whose deletion leaves a word equal to the identity. Proposition 2.1 shows that these deletion certificates are exactly equivalent to products of conjugates of generators: deleting the marked letters gives one inequality, and conversely the subwords between deleted letters telescope to express the original element as a product of conjugates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching of inverse letters can be treated as a strengthened deletion/cancellation certificate: instead of merely deleting letters, one records which inverse letters cancel. This suggests converting questions about the existence or structure of matchings into inductive statements about trivialising sequences in a free group.

proof_techniques.name: Balanced-relation invariance
proof_techniques.description: The balanced-relation framework gives a robust way to show that cancellation length is unchanged under local substitutions in a presentation. If a relation v = w is balanced, then every deletion pattern on one side can be transferred to a deletion pattern of the same size on the other side. Lemma 2.2 and Proposition 2.5 use this to prove that, in presentations all of whose relations are balanced, cancellation length depends only on the represented group element and not on the representative word.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For free groups, where reductions are governed only by inverse cancellation, this supports simplifying representatives or subwords while preserving cancellation data. If a proof of contractibility needs local word modifications before constructing or comparing matchings, the balanced-relation argument is the paper’s mechanism for justifying that such modifications do not change the relevant cancellation length.

proof_techniques.name: First-letter dichotomy and splitting over an inverse occurrence
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.10 is the central free-group technique. In a minimal trivialisation of xw, either the initial letter x is deleted, or it survives and must cancel with a specific surviving inverse occurrence x^{-1} inside w. In the latter case, freeness forces the word between x and that x^{-1} to be trivialised independently, and also forces the suffix after that occurrence to be trivialised independently. This converts a global cancellation problem into two smaller interval problems.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a reducible word of norm zero, the delete-the-first-letter alternative cannot realise the zero minimum, so some inverse occurrence gives a decomposition w = u x^{-1} v with both u and v reducible. Iterating this gives a nested noncrossing inverse-letter cancellation pairing, which is a natural base object for inductive or discrete-Morse approaches to the matching complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Dynamic programming over connected subwords
proof_techniques.description: The algorithm in Section 2.9 organises the first-letter recurrence by connected subwords. Norms of all shorter intervals are computed first and stored; the value for a longer interval is obtained by scanning for inverse occurrences of its first letter and reading off the two smaller interval values. Thus all needed information is local to intervals, and the recurrence has an explicitly triangular dependence order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are built from pairings of positions in a linear word, and resolving or choosing a pair often separates the word into intervals. The paper’s interval-recursion suggests trying an inductive cover, filtration, or acyclic matching on F_w whose pieces are indexed by subwords and whose attaching data decomposes as products or joins over smaller intervals.

proof_techniques.name: Forced cancellation by cost barriers
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 3.6, the paper uses a quantitative free-group cancellation argument to force a particular letter to appear in every minimal trivialising sequence. The constructed word contains a long block b^{4k} a^{-1} b^{-4k}; if a^{-1} is not deleted, then pairing it with an earlier a would require deleting more than 2k intervening b-letters, which is more expensive than simply deleting a^{-1}. This kind of estimate shows that sufficiently long separator blocks can make a local cancellation choice unavoidable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem fixes an arbitrary word rather than allowing auxiliary long blocks, the same pattern may help prove that certain local resolutions or pair choices are forced under additional filtrations or auxiliary constructions. It is a method for converting free-group reduced-length estimates into rigidity statements about cancellation patterns.

other_useful_info: The paper’s term trivialising sequence means a sequence of indices, or equivalently the corresponding letters, whose deletion makes the word trivial. In the free-group algorithm, the proof works with reduced connected subwords and stores only their norm values. Remark 2.11 notes that outside special settings there is no obvious general algorithm for the conjugation-invariant norm; for groups with balanced presentations and solvable word problem one can in principle compute by checking subsequences, but this is exponential in time. A direct consequence of Lemma 2.10, not separately named in the paper, is that any nonempty freely trivial word xw admits a decomposition w = u x^{-1} v with both u and v freely trivial; iterating this is the standard nested cancellation-pairing construction for trivial words in a free group.
why_relevant: Develops foldings and cancellation pairings for words in free groups, including algorithms based on matching inverse letters. This is useful for translating reducible words into structured cancellation diagrams.
